metadata.summary: Decision framework for Django: framework selection, API frameworks, architecture patterns, databases, deployment.

content.title: Django Decision Tree
content.summary: A comprehensive decision framework for Django development. Covers framework selection, architecture patterns, API framework choices, database selection, deployment strategies, and third-party package evaluation.

why.p: Making the right architectural decisions early in a Django project prevents costly refactoring later. This methodology provides structured decision trees for: Framework Selection - When to choose Django vs alternatives; API Framework Choice - DRF vs Django Ninja vs Django views; Architecture Patterns - Service layer, clean architecture, DDD; Database Selection - PostgreSQL vs MySQL vs SQLite; Deployment Strategy - VPS vs PaaS vs Kubernetes vs Serverless; Third-Party Packages - Evaluation criteria and recommendations; Code Organization - Where to place code in Django projects.

Making the right choices early prevents costly refactoring later. Django's batteries included philosophy can lead to over-engineering simple projects or under-architecting large ones. A structured decision framework accelerates consensus and reduces post-hoc regret.

list.item: Starting a new Django project and choosing between Django and other frameworks.
Selecting API framework (DRF, Django Ninja, raw views) for new endpoint family.
Evaluating architecture patterns when Django repo has grown past 50 models.
Choosing database backend (PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite) based on project characteristics.
Planning deployment strategy (VPS, PaaS, Kubernetes, Serverless).
Evaluating third-party packages for quality, maintenance, compatibility, security.
Code-placement reviews during code review of Django PRs (which directory does this go in).
Onboarding developers to project architecture and explaining design rationale.
After spec sign-off with deployment chosen — re-running the tree wastes cycles; only trigger on scope change.
When the choice is already constrained by infra/team policy (e.g. must be on Django + Postgres + k8s); skip the framework branch.
Tiny scripts, ETL jobs, or single-page admin tools — Django is overkill; the tree's smallest leaf still implies app/project structure.
Greenfield projects where the bottleneck is product-market fit, not architecture; fat models, simple views wins, no decision tree needed.
For a single feature inside a Django project — use code-placement guidance only, not the full tree.

section.name: framework-selection
section.p: The first decision: when to use Django vs FastAPI, Flask, or other Python frameworks.

rule.statement: Use Django when: building a web application (not CLI-only), needing an ORM, requiring an admin interface, managing user accounts and permissions, handling complex forms, or requiring server-side rendering. Consider FastAPI when: building API-only services, prioritizing async-first design, needing minimal dependencies, or the team prefers the FastAPI ecosystem.
rule.rationale: Django's batteries-included approach makes full-featured web applications quick to prototype but adds overhead for minimal APIs. FastAPI excels at async-first, type-hinted APIs but requires manual setup for admin, auth, and ORM integration.

section.p: Framework Selection Scenarios: Full-featured web app with admin = Django. API-only, high concurrency = FastAPI. Django + FastAPI-like API syntax = Django Ninja. Microservices, async-first = FastAPI. CMS with content editing = Django + Wagtail. Simple REST API = Django Ninja or FastAPI.

section.name: api-framework-selection
section.p: If choosing Django, which API framework to pair it with: Django REST Framework (DRF), Django Ninja, or raw Django views.

rule.statement: Use DRF when: the existing codebase uses DRF, needing ViewSets for standard CRUD, requiring an extensive serializer ecosystem, or prioritizing community resources. Use Django Ninja when: starting a new project, prioritizing performance and modern type hints, preferring FastAPI-like DX, or needing auto-generated OpenAPI docs without extra packages.
rule.rationale: DRF dominates market share and has mature third-party packages (drf-spectacular, rest-framework-simplejwt). Django Ninja is newer and lighter, with faster performance due to reduced serialization overhead and native async support.

section.p: API Framework Selection: Full CRUD with ViewSets = DRF. Performance-critical API = Ninja. Auto OpenAPI docs = Ninja or DRF + drf-spectacular. Large existing DRF ecosystem = DRF. Modern type hints + async = Ninja. GraphQL = Graphene-Django or Strawberry.

section.name: architecture-patterns
section.p: Choosing the code organization pattern: Simple (Fat Models), Service Layer, or Clean Architecture / DDD-inspired.

rule.statement: Use Simple (Fat Models) for: small projects (less than 5 apps, less than 10 models), MVPs, prototypes, or 1-2 person teams. Use Service Layer for: medium projects (5-15 apps, 10-50 models), growing teams, multiple entry points (API + CLI + web), and moderate business logic. Use Clean Architecture / DDD for: large enterprise projects (more than 15 apps, more than 50 models), long-term maintenance priority, complex domain logic, or multiple bounded contexts.
rule.rationale: Simple architecture reduces boilerplate for small projects but becomes unmaintainable as complexity grows. Service Layer keeps business logic testable and reusable across entry points. Clean Architecture enforces isolation and is necessary for large teams working on distinct domains.

section.p: Architecture Pattern Selection: Small (less than 5 apps) = Fat models, simple views. Medium (5-15 apps) = Service layer pattern. Large (greater than 15 apps) = Clean architecture / DDD-inspired. Microservices = Domain-driven design.

section.name: database-selection
section.p: Choosing the database backend: PostgreSQL, MySQL, or SQLite.

rule.statement: Use PostgreSQL for: production environments, complex queries (CTEs, window functions), full-text search, JSON/JSONB fields, geographic data (PostGIS), or high write concurrency. Use SQLite for: development/testing, read-heavy workloads, single-server deployment, side projects/prototypes, or simplicity priority. Use MySQL for: existing MySQL infrastructure, team MySQL expertise, simple queries, or AWS RDS MySQL preference.
rule.rationale: PostgreSQL provides advanced features and strong Django integration ideal for production. SQLite offers development simplicity and zero-configuration deployment for small projects. MySQL serves legacy infrastructure and simpler applications.

section.p: Database Selection Scenarios: Production, complex queries = PostgreSQL. Simple apps, side projects = SQLite (with Litestream). Legacy MySQL infrastructure = MySQL 8+. Geographic applications = PostgreSQL + PostGIS. Development/testing = SQLite.

section.name: deployment-options
section.p: Choosing the deployment platform: PaaS, VPS, Kubernetes, or Serverless.

rule.statement: Use PaaS (Render, Fly.io, Railway) for: simplicity, managed infrastructure, small teams with limited DevOps expertise, and rapid iteration. Use VPS (DigitalOcean, Hetzner) for: full control, cost optimization at scale, custom infrastructure, or team DevOps experience. Use Kubernetes for: horizontal scaling, zero-downtime deployments, high-availability requirements, or microservices architecture. Use Serverless for: highly variable traffic, cost optimization, stateless applications, or event-driven architecture.
rule.rationale: PaaS trades control for simplicity and speed. VPS provides control and cost efficiency but requires infrastructure management. Kubernetes handles scaling and reliability but adds operational complexity. Serverless optimizes for cost and scaling but adds cold-start latency.

section.p: Deployment Options: Simplicity, managed = PaaS (Render, Fly.io, Railway). Full control = VPS (DigitalOcean, Hetzner). Enterprise, scaling = Kubernetes. Cost optimization = Serverless (Cloud Run, Lambda). Static sites + API = Vercel/Netlify + Django API.

section.name: code-placement
section.p: Deciding where to place code within a Django project structure.

rule.statement: Use this decision tree: (1) Changes database? → services/. (2) External API call? → services/ or integrations/. (3) Pure function? → utils/. (4) HTTP handling? → views/. (5) Data structure? → models/. (6) Input validation? → serializers/. (7) Background task? → tasks/. (8) Reusable? → core/ or common/.
rule.rationale: This hierarchy enforces testability (pure functions in utils), side effects isolation (services), and dependency direction (views depend on services, not vice versa).

section.code: What does the function do? | +-> Changes database? -> services/ +-> External API call? -> services/ or integrations/ +-> Pure function? -> utils/ +-> HTTP handling? -> views/ +-> Data structure? -> models/ +-> Input validation? -> serializers/ +-> Background task? -> tasks/ +-> Reusable? -> core/ or common/

section.name: package-evaluation
section.p: Criteria for evaluating and choosing third-party Django packages. Before adding a package, verify: (1) Maintenance — commits within 6 months; (2) Compatibility — Django 5.x + Python 3.11+ support; (3) Community — GitHub stars and PyPI downloads; (4) Documentation — clear and complete; (5) Test coverage — CI passing, good coverage; (6) Security — no known vulnerabilities; (7) License — compatible with your project; (8) Necessity — cannot be done with Django built-ins.

rule.statement: Before adding a package, verify: recent maintenance, Django 5.x compatibility, community signals (GitHub stars, PyPI downloads), documentation quality, test coverage, no known security vulnerabilities, license compatibility, and that it cannot be done with Django built-ins.
rule.rationale: Unmaintained, incompatible, or poorly documented packages create maintenance debt. Verify these signals before adding the dependency.

section.p: Package Risk Assessment: Low = greater than 5k stars, active, documented. Medium = 1-5k stars, maintained. High = less than 1k stars, no recent commits. Critical = abandoned, security issues.

Essential Packages (2025): API tools include django-rest-framework (REST API) and django-ninja (FastAPI-like API). Debug tools include django-debug-toolbar (debug panel). Auth tools include django-allauth (authentication). CORS tools include django-cors-headers. Filtering tools include django-filter (query filtering). Task tools include celery (background tasks). Environment tools include django-environ (environment variables). Storage tools include django-storages (cloud storage).

section.name: version-compatibility
section.p: Recommended minimum versions for Django ecosystem components in 2025: Django 5.0+, Python 3.11+, PostgreSQL 14+, MySQL 8.0+, DRF 3.15+, Django Ninja 1.0+.

section.name: llm-usage-tips
section.p: How to effectively use LLMs to assist with Django architecture decisions.

rule.statement: When asking an LLM for Django architecture advice: (1) provide specific project context (team size, timeline, budget, expected scale); (2) state your constraints clearly (must support PostgreSQL, existing Django codebase, etc.); (3) rank trade-off priorities (developer experience vs performance vs cost); (4) include relevant code snippets; (5) ask for concrete examples in the response.
rule.rationale: Context-rich prompts yield better recommendations than generic questions. The LLM needs to understand your specific situation to offer targeted advice rather than generic best practices.

example.note: Too vague — no context about project size, team, or constraints.

example.code: I'm building a B2C e-commerce platform with Django. - Team: 5 developers (3 with Django experience) - Timeline: 6 months to MVP - Expected scale: 10k daily active users - Need: admin panel, mobile API, complex inventory logic Should I use DRF or Django Ninja? What architecture pattern?

list.item: Ask for architectural decision records (ADRs) explaining trade-offs, not just recommendations.
Request code examples for each recommendation, not just prose explanations.
Verify currency — LLM recommendations may be outdated; ask for recent changes to tools or frameworks.
Avoid asking what's best without context; instead ask which is better for my situation.
Include team experience level — recommendations differ for experienced teams vs beginners.

section.name: key-principles
section.p: Core principles guiding Django architecture decisions: Single responsibility — each module has one clear purpose. Dependency direction — views depend on services, not vice versa. Testability — pure functions in utils, side effects in services. Explicit boundaries — clear separation between layers and apps. Predictable location — any team member can find any code. Framework leverage — use Django's batteries before reinventing.
